questions.question:
Which of the following decision problems are undecidable?
I. Given NFAs N_1 and N_2, is L(N_1) ∩ L(N_2) = ∅?
II. Given a CFG G and a string w, does w ∈ L(G)?
III. Given CFGs G_1 and G_2, is L(G_1) ∩ L(G_2) = ∅?
IV. Given a TM M, is L(M) = ∅?

A. I and IV only
B. II and III only
C. III and IV only
D. II and IV only
questions.to_find: Which of the four given decision problems are undecidable

understand.plain: A decision problem is undecidable if no algorithm can always correctly answer yes/no. We need to classify each of four problems about NFAs, CFGs, and TMs as decidable or undecidable. Regular languages have very powerful decidable properties, while for CFGs some properties become undecidable, and for TMs even more become undecidable.

keywords.explain: A problem for which a Turing machine always halts with the correct yes/no answer.
keywords.example: Is w in L(NFA)? — yes, simulate the NFA on w.

keywords.explain: A problem for which no Turing machine can always produce a correct yes/no answer in finite time.
keywords.example: Whether L(M) = ∅ for an arbitrary TM M cannot be decided by any algorithm.

keywords.explain: Checking if two CFLs share any common string; CFLs are NOT closed under intersection, and this problem is undecidable.
keywords.example: L(G1) ∩ L(G2) = ∅ cannot be decided algorithmically.

understand.visual_alt: Table comparing four problems: I (NFA intersection empty = decidable), II (CFG membership = decidable), III (CFG intersection empty = undecidable), IV (TM empty = undecidable). III and IV are highlighted.

given.aim: Identify which of the four decision problems cannot be solved algorithmically (undecidable).

terms.meaning: Check if two regular languages have no common string.
terms.example: NFA accepts even-length strings; another accepts odd-length — intersection is empty.
terms.connects: Regular languages are closed under intersection; emptiness of a regular language is decidable, so this is decidable.

terms.meaning: Membership test: does the grammar generate the given string?
terms.example: G generates a^n b^n; is 'aabb' in L(G)? Yes.
terms.connects: CYK algorithm decides CFG membership in O(n^3), so it is decidable.

terms.meaning: Check if two context-free languages share any word.
terms.example: G1 generates a^n b^n c^m, G2 generates a^m b^n c^n — intersection is {a^n b^n c^n}.
terms.connects: This problem reduces from Post Correspondence Problem (PCP), making it undecidable.

terms.meaning: Check if a Turing machine accepts no strings at all.
terms.example: A TM that only loops forever accepts nothing; determining this is undecidable.
terms.connects: By Rice's theorem, any non-trivial semantic property of TMs is undecidable.

given.plan: Classify each problem by the automaton class involved. For regular languages (NFA), most properties including intersection emptiness are decidable. For CFGs, membership is decidable but intersection emptiness is undecidable. For TMs, emptiness is undecidable by Rice's theorem.

steps.title: Analyze Problem I — NFA Intersection Emptiness
steps.formula_raw: Regular languages closed under ∩; emptiness of DFA/NFA is decidable
steps.apply: Given NFAs N1 and N2, construct product NFA/DFA for L(N1) ∩ L(N2), then check emptiness using reachability. Both operations are algorithmic and terminate. Hence Problem I is DECIDABLE.
steps.note: Emptiness of a regular language is decidable because we only need to check if any accepting state is reachable from the start state.

steps.title: Analyze Problem II — CFG Membership
steps.formula_raw: CYK algorithm decides w ∈ L(G) in O(|w|^3 * |G|)
steps.apply: Given CFG G and string w, the CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm fills a parse table in polynomial time and decides whether G can derive w. Hence Problem II is DECIDABLE.
steps.note: CFG membership is decidable even though many other CFG properties are not.

steps.title: Analyze Problem III — CFG Intersection Emptiness
steps.formula_raw: L(G1) ∩ L(G2) = ∅ reduces from Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)
steps.apply: CFLs are NOT closed under intersection. Furthermore, determining whether L(G1) ∩ L(G2) is empty reduces to PCP (Post Correspondence Problem), which is known to be undecidable. So no algorithm can solve this for all CFGs. Hence Problem III is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: Even though emptiness of a single CFL is decidable, the intersection of two CFLs may not even be context-free, and checking its emptiness is undecidable.

steps.title: Analyze Problem IV — TM Emptiness
steps.formula_raw: Rice's Theorem: any non-trivial property of L(TM) is undecidable
steps.apply: The property 'L(M) = ∅' is a non-trivial property of the language recognized by M (some TMs have empty language, some don't). By Rice's theorem, it is undecidable. We can also reduce H_TM (halting problem) to it. Hence Problem IV is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: This is the TM language emptiness problem — not to be confused with the universal TM problem.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Decidable: I and II. Undecidable: III and IV. This matches option C.
steps.note: The answer is C: III and IV only.

solution.result: C. III and IV only — Problems III (CFG intersection emptiness) and IV (TM language emptiness) are undecidable.

questions.formula_note: Rice's theorem (any non-trivial semantic property of TMs is undecidable) and the reducibility from PCP to CFG intersection emptiness.

questions.question:
Let Sigma and Delta be finite alphabets and let # be a symbol outside both Sigma and Delta. Let f be a total function from Sigma* to Delta*. We say f is computable if there exists a Turing machine which given an input w in Sigma*, always halts with f(w) on its tape. Let L_f denote the language { w#f(w) | w in Sigma* }. Which of the following statements is true?

A. f is computable if and only if L_f is recursive (decidable)
B. f is computable if and only if L_f is recursively enumerable
C. If f is computable then L_f is recursive, but not conversely
D. If L_f is recursively enumerable then f is computable, but not conversely
questions.answer: A. f is computable if and only if L_f is recursive (decidable)
questions.to_find: The correct relationship between computability of function f and the decidability class of language L_f

understand.plain: A total computable function f always halts on every input and produces an output. The language L_f consists of all pairs w#f(w) — the input word, a separator #, and the correct output. We want to know exactly which language class L_f belongs to when f is computable, and vice versa. Since f is total and computable (always halts), L_f should be more than just RE — it should be recursive.

keywords.explain: A function computed by a TM that halts on every input, producing the correct output.
keywords.example: f(w) = reverse(w) is computable — TM always halts with the reverse.

keywords.explain: A language for which a TM always halts (accepts or rejects) for every input.
keywords.example: All regular and context-free languages are recursive.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a TM that may loop forever on non-member inputs.
keywords.example: The halting language is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings of the form input-separator-output for function f.
keywords.example: If f maps 'ab' to 'ba', then 'ab#ba' is in L_f.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing: total computable function f on Sigma* → L_f = {w#f(w)} is a recursive (decidable) language, and conversely. Both directions hold because f is total.

given.aim: Determine the exact relationship between f being computable (total) and L_f being in a language complexity class.

terms.term: f: Sigma* -> Delta* total and computable
terms.meaning: TM M_f halts on every w in Sigma* with f(w) on tape.
terms.example: f(w) = length of w written in unary — always halts.
terms.connects: If f is total computable, we can decide membership in L_f: compute f(w), check if the string after # matches.

terms.meaning: Encoded input-output pairs of f with separator #.
terms.example: If f(a) = bb, then a#bb ∈ L_f.
terms.connects: Membership in L_f can be decided by parsing the input into w and u (split at #), then running f on w and checking if result = u.

terms.meaning: Recursive = TM always halts; RE = TM halts only on yes instances.
terms.example: Halting language is RE but not recursive.
terms.connects: Since f is total, the TM for L_f always halts; so L_f is recursive, not just RE.

given.plan: Show both directions: (1) if f is computable (total), then L_f is decidable (recursive); (2) if L_f is recursive, then f is computable. Then compare with the four options.

steps.title: Forward direction: f computable (total) => L_f is recursive
steps.apply: Build a TM M that on input x: (a) check if x has exactly one # symbol; reject if not. (b) Split x = w#u. (c) Run M_f on w — since f is total, M_f always halts with f(w). (d) Accept iff u = f(w). Since M_f always halts and comparison always halts, M always halts (accept or reject). So L_f is recursive.
steps.note: The key is that f is TOTAL — M_f never loops, so the decider for L_f also never loops.

steps.title: Reverse direction: L_f is recursive => f is computable
steps.apply: Suppose we have a decider D for L_f. To compute f(w) for any w: enumerate all strings u in Delta* in lexicographic order. For each u, run D on w#u. Since L_f contains exactly one pair w#f(w), D will eventually accept for u = f(w) and reject for all other u. However, D always halts, so each check terminates. This procedure computes f(w) and always halts. Hence f is computable.
steps.note: The enumeration terminates because D always halts (L_f is recursive, not just RE).

steps.title: Check the options
steps.apply: Both directions hold: f computable <=> L_f is recursive. This matches option A exactly. Option B claims L_f is RE (weaker than recursive), which would be incorrect since we proved it is actually recursive. Options C and D claim only one direction, which are also incorrect.
steps.note: If L_f were only RE (not recursive), the reverse direction would fail because the enumeration might loop.

solution.result: A. f is computable if and only if L_f is recursive (decidable).

questions.formula_note: Equivalence between total computable functions and decidable (recursive) languages via encoding; the totality of f ensures the decider for L_f always halts.

questions.question:
Let L(R) be the language represented by regular expression R. Let L(G) be the language generated by a context free grammar G. Let L(M) be the language accepted by a Turing machine M. Which of the following decision problems are undecidable?
I. Given a regular expression R and a string w, is w in L(R)?
II. Given a context-free grammar G, is L(G) = Sigma* for some alphabet Sigma?
III. Given a Turing machine M and a string w, is w in L(M)?

A. I and II only
B. III only
C. II and III only
D. III and IV only (I, II and III)
questions.to_find: Which of the three decision problems about regular expressions, CFGs, and TMs are undecidable

understand.plain: We have three problems: checking if a string belongs to a regular language (easy), checking if a CFG generates all strings (surprisingly hard — undecidable), and checking if a TM accepts a particular string (undecidable). We need to find which are undecidable. Problem I is straightforward because regular languages are easy to decide. Problems II and III are undecidable.

keywords.explain: Checking if a string matches a regular expression — can be done by converting to DFA.
keywords.example: Is 'aab' matched by (a|b)*? Yes, since (a|b)* matches any string over {a,b}.

keywords.explain: Checking if a CFG generates ALL strings over its alphabet — this is undecidable.
keywords.example: L(G) = Sigma* means every possible string is in the language.

keywords.explain: Given a TM and a string, does the TM accept that string? This is undecidable (halting problem variant).
keywords.example: A_TM = {<M,w> | M accepts w} is undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: Three problems: I (regex membership = decidable), II (CFG universality = undecidable), III (TM acceptance = undecidable). II and III are undecidable.

given.aim: Classify each of the three decision problems as decidable or undecidable.

terms.connects: Convert R to DFA, simulate on w — always terminates. DECIDABLE.

terms.meaning: CFG universality — does G generate every possible string?
terms.example: G with rules S -> aSb | epsilon does NOT generate all strings.
terms.connects: Complement of L(G) is the set of strings NOT in L(G). Checking emptiness of complement is undecidable. UNDECIDABLE.

terms.meaning: TM acceptance — does TM M accept string w?
terms.example: Does TM for halting problem accept <M,w>?
terms.connects: This is exactly A_TM, the acceptance problem for TMs, which is undecidable (reduces from halting problem). UNDECIDABLE.

given.plan: Decide each problem: I is decidable by DFA simulation; II is undecidable (CFG universality is undecidable); III is undecidable (A_TM is undecidable). The answer is the set {II, III}.

steps.title: Problem I: Regular expression membership
steps.formula_raw: Convert R to NFA to DFA, simulate on w
steps.apply: Given R and w, convert R to an NFA using Thompson's construction, then convert to DFA, then simulate the DFA on w. This entire process is algorithmic and always terminates with accept or reject. Problem I is DECIDABLE.
steps.note: Regular language membership is one of the most basic decidable problems in formal language theory.

steps.title: Problem II: CFG universality
steps.formula_raw: L(G) = Sigma* iff complement of L(G) is empty
steps.apply: The problem of deciding whether L(G) = Sigma* for a CFG G is undecidable. This can be proved by reduction from the halting problem or from Post Correspondence Problem. Even though emptiness of a single CFL is decidable, universality (checking if the complement is empty) is not. Problem II is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: Context-free languages are not closed under complement, so we cannot simply complement the grammar and check emptiness.

steps.title: Problem III: TM acceptance
steps.formula_raw: A_TM = {<M,w> | M accepts w} is undecidable
steps.apply: Deciding whether TM M accepts string w is the acceptance problem A_TM, which is undecidable. It is shown undecidable by reduction from the halting problem (or by diagonalization). A_TM is RE (we can run M on w and if M accepts we know) but not recursive. Problem III is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: A_TM is perhaps the most fundamental undecidable problem; all other TM undecidability results reduce from it or the halting problem.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Decidable: I. Undecidable: II and III. The answer matching this is option C.
steps.note: Answer: C — II and III only.

solution.result: C. II and III only — CFG universality (Problem II) and TM acceptance (Problem III) are undecidable.

questions.formula_note: Undecidability of A_TM (TM acceptance) via diagonalization; undecidability of CFG universality via reduction from PCP.

questions.question:
Consider the following problems. L(G) denotes the language generated by a grammar G. L(M) denotes the language accepted by a machine M.
I. For an unrestricted grammar G and a string w, whether w in L(G)
II. Given a Turing machine M, whether L(M) is regular
III. Given two context-free grammars G_1 and G_2, whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
IV. Given an NFA N, whether there is a deterministic PDA P such that L(N) = L(P)

Which of the following is correct?

A. Only I and II are undecidable
B. Only II is undecidable
C. Only I, II and III are undecidable
D. Only I and III are undecidable
questions.answer: C. Only I, II and III are undecidable
questions.to_find: Which of the four problems about unrestricted grammar, TM regularity, CFL equivalence, and NFA vs DPDA are undecidable

understand.plain: We have four problems involving different types of grammars and machines. Problem I asks about membership in a language generated by an unrestricted (Type 0) grammar, which is equivalent to TM acceptance — undecidable. Problem II asks if a TM's language is regular — undecidable by Rice's theorem. Problem III asks if two CFLs are equal — undecidable. Problem IV asks if an NFA and a deterministic PDA accept the same language — this is decidable because regular languages are a proper subset of DCFLs and NFA languages are regular, so we can check equivalence.

keywords.explain: A grammar with no restrictions on production rules; equivalent in power to Turing machines.
keywords.example: Productions like AB -> BA are allowed in unrestricted grammars.

keywords.explain: Any non-trivial property of the language recognized by a Turing machine is undecidable.
keywords.example: 'Is L(M) regular?' is a non-trivial language property, so it is undecidable.

keywords.explain: Checking if two CFLs are equal; undecidable by reduction from PCP.
keywords.example: L(G1) = L(G2) cannot be decided algorithmically for arbitrary CFGs.

keywords.explain: A pushdown automaton where the next move is uniquely determined at each step; accepts DCFLs.
keywords.example: The language {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is accepted by a DPDA.

understand.visual_alt: Four problems: I (unrestricted grammar membership = undecidable, equivalent to TM acceptance), II (TM language regular? = undecidable by Rice), III (CFL equivalence = undecidable), IV (NFA = DPDA? = decidable since all regular languages are DCFLs).

given.aim: Determine which among the four given decision problems are undecidable.

terms.term: Problem I: w ∈ L(G) for unrestricted G
terms.meaning: Membership in a Type-0 grammar language.
terms.example: Unrestricted grammars generate exactly the RE languages.
Is the language accepted by a TM a regular language like (a|b)*?
terms.connects: Non-trivial language property of TM => undecidable by Rice's theorem.

terms.term: Problem III: L(G1) = L(G2) for CFGs
terms.example: Do two different grammars generate exactly the same set of strings?

terms.term: Problem IV: L(N) = L(P) for NFA N and DPDA P
terms.meaning: Check if a regular language equals the language of a deterministic PDA.
terms.example: Every regular language is also a DCFL (accepted by some DPDA).
terms.connects: Regular languages ⊂ DCFL; and DPDA language equivalence with regular language is decidable because we can complement a DPDA and check emptiness of symmetric difference.

given.plan: Analyze each problem: I is RE membership (undecidable); II is a TM language property (undecidable by Rice); III is CFL equivalence (undecidable); IV involves NFA vs DPDA which is decidable (regular languages are a subset of DCFLs, and we can decide equivalence).

steps.title: Problem I: Unrestricted grammar membership
steps.apply: Unrestricted (Type 0) grammars generate exactly the recursively enumerable (RE) languages — the same class as TM-acceptable languages. Membership in an RE language is undecidable (equivalent to A_TM). Problem I is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: If it were decidable, we could decide the halting problem by constructing an unrestricted grammar for H_TM.

steps.title: Problem II: Is L(M) regular?
steps.formula_raw: Rice's theorem: any non-trivial property of L(TM) is undecidable
steps.apply: 'L(M) is regular' is a property of L(M). It is non-trivial: some TMs have regular languages (e.g., TM for emptyset), others don't (e.g., TM for {a^n b^n}). By Rice's theorem, this property is undecidable. Problem II is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: Rice's theorem applies to all non-trivial semantic properties of TM languages.

steps.title: Problem III: CFL equivalence
steps.formula_raw: L(G1) = L(G2) reduces from Post Correspondence Problem
steps.apply: Deciding whether two CFLs are equal is undecidable. This is proved by reduction from PCP (Post Correspondence Problem). Even though CFG emptiness and membership are decidable, equivalence of two CFGs is not. Problem III is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: This is different from DFA/NFA equivalence which is decidable by minimization and isomorphism.

steps.title: Problem IV: L(N) = L(P) for NFA N and DPDA P
steps.formula_raw: Every regular language is a DCFL; DPDA complement is also a DPDA
steps.apply: L(N) is a regular language (since N is an NFA). Every regular language is also a DCFL. Given L(N) is regular and L(P) is a DCFL, we need to check their equality. DCFLs are closed under complement, so we can compute symmetric difference. In fact, since L(N) is regular, we can construct DFA for L(N) and complement the DPDA P; check if L(N) - L(P) = ∅ and L(P) - L(N) = ∅. Both checks are decidable. Problem IV is DECIDABLE.
steps.note: The key insight is that regular languages are a strict subset of DCFLs and both are closed under complement, making equivalence decidable in this restricted case.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Undecidable: I, II, III. Decidable: IV. This matches option C: 'Only I, II and III are undecidable'.

solution.result: C. Only I, II and III are undecidable. Problem IV (NFA vs DPDA equivalence) is decidable.

questions.formula_note: Rice's theorem for Problems I and II; reducibility from Post Correspondence Problem for Problems I and III; decidability of regular-vs-DCFL equivalence using closure properties for Problem IV.
